checks.a_analysis: The output JSON faithfully reproduces the text found in the source PDF. All headers and body text (titles, dates, legend items, sources, and contact info) correspond directly to text visible in the source document. No invented content was found.

I verified the three email addresses listed on page 3 of the source PDF ('katie.doherty@rutherfordcountync.gov', 'relkins@sosnc.gov', and 'elizabeth.daniel@nc.gov') against the output JSON. They match exactly. No phone numbers or URLs were present in the source.
All three pages of the source PDF are represented in the output JSON. Page 1 (Title page), Page 2 (Map and Legend), and Page 3 (Thank you/Contacts) are all present with their respective content.
The source document does not contain any formal tables. The legend on page 2 is represented as a paragraph list, which is an acceptable structural choice.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: No significant text omissions were identified. The complex information on page 2 (the legend and source attribution) was captured completely. The contact list on page 3 is also complete.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: There are no differences in wording, numbers, or dates between the source and the output. The date 'October 7, 2025' and all statistical counts in the map legend match the original exactly.

checks.a_analysis: Each slide's content is captured within its respective section. While the date 'October 7, 2025' appears in both Section 1 and Section 2, this is a reflection of the source document where the date is repeated on each slide. Similarly, the logos appear once per slide in the source and are represented in the output sections. There are no instances of a text block appearing more times in the output than in the source.

The output uses a flat hierarchy where each slide's main title is promoted to a section title. This is an appropriate structural choice for a slide-based document. No hierarchy skips or incorrect nesting are present.
There are no tables in the source document.

The reading order in the output correctly follows the logical flow of the slides. Section 1 covers the cover slide, Section 2 covers the map and its legend/sources, and Section 3 covers the concluding contact information. Within each section, the sequence from title to paragraphs to images follows the source's visual and logical structure.

The legend on Page 2 and the contact list on Page 3 are rendered as paragraphs with newlines or semicolons respectively. This accurately preserves the meaning and relationship of the items as presented in the source.
The output correctly identifies the three email addresses on Page 3 and provides valid mailto: links for each. No URLs were fabricated or missed.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output correctly transforms the multi-slide PDF presentation into a continuous vertical layout. The visual organization follows the logical flow of the original document: starting with the title and date (from slide 1), followed by the project status map and legend (from slide 2), and concluding with the contact information (from slide 3). The spatial relationship of elements is preserved in a manner appropriate for web viewing.
The source PDF does not contain any tables; therefore, there are no tables to misrender in the HTML output.

All meaningful images from the source PDF are present in the HTML output. This includes the large 'NCDIT' logo from the first page, the NC Parcels map with its associated legend and logos (NCGICC) from the second page, and the logo block at the end of the document. The images are rendered clearly and in their correct sequence.

There is no visual duplication of body content. While text from within the map image (title, legend, sources) is rendered as live text in the HTML above the image, this is an accessibility feature (providing a text alternative) and does not constitute a defect in content duplication. The core content blocks from each slide appear exactly once in the correct sequence.
